text: The patient is placed in left lateral decubitus position, slightly bent in the mid thoracic region to create a semi-prone exposure.
The right lung is desufflated and the exposure on the ozygous vein is established.
By rotating the camera to caudal, the diaphragm is visualised.
The inferior pulmonary ligament is lifted and transected.
The transection is continued cranially through the right parietal pleura.
Here, the periesophageal lymph tissue is dissected over the pericardium for an on-block resection.
the oesophagus, with a transparent left pleura covering the left lung.
Below, the pericardium is visible.
The transection of the right parietal pleura is continued cranially, towards the ozygous vein.
The arcus of the ozygous vein is lifted, clipped, and subsequently transected.
The transection of the right parietal pleura is continued in the cranial direction, starting from the ozygous vein and continuing to the apex of the thoracic cavity.
Near the apex, the superior vena cava is visualized.
Starting at the apex of the thoracic cavity, the transection is continued through the left parietal pleura back to the zygus vein.
The previously transected right parietal pleura is encountered.
The paratracheal dissection starts at the transected right parietal pleura.
Care should be taken not to injure the vagus nerve that runs directly under the dissection plane, the trachea, and on the right side of it, the vagus nerve.
Whilst dissecting, the left recurrent laryngial nerve should be identified and preserved.
The dissection is continued over the left visceral pleura until the previously dissected part is encountered.
Great care must be taken not to perforate the pleura.
Here, the left lung is seen again through the thin pleural lining.
The oesophagus is lifted and the dissection is continued on the right side.
At this point, the dissection of the paratracheal tissue is complete.
The transection of the left parietal pleura is continued along the border of the ozygous vein towards the caudal side.
The right bronchial artery is identified at a typical location where it crosses the oesophagus cranially to the ozygous vein.
The right bronchial artery is transected.
The transection of the left parietal pleura is continued towards the diaphragm.
In this orientation, the ozygous vein is used as a landmark to start the mesoesophageal dissection.
Here, the aorta is visualised.
All the branches from the oesophagus running towards the ozygous vein are transected.
the aorta with on the right side of it the thoracic duct.
The thoracic duct is isolated near the diaphragm.
Note its surrounding fat sheath should be kept intact to prevent chile leakage.
The thoracic duct should be kept with the mesoesophagus for an on-block removal.
The thoracic duct is transected near the diaphragm.
It is transected together with its surrounding fat sheath.
This is the lumen of the thoracic duct, including the surrounding fat sheath.
Any aorta-esophageal arteries that are encountered will be clipped and transected.
While dissecting away from the aorta, care should be taken not to puncture the left pleura.
An accidental defect is made in the pleura, exposing the left lung.
When continuing the dissection, try to keep the defect as small as possible.
The oesophagus is lifted and is dissected further until the dissection is completed.
With a Penrose drain, it can be encircled and lifted, thus facilitating the en bloc resection of the mesoesophagus.
Then the dissection is continued at the left side over the pericardium.
During the dissection of the mesoesophagus from the pericard and the pulmonary veins, the right plane should be followed.
Keep the dissection superficial to prevent damage to the herefore mentioned structures.
The border of the left pleura is encountered and the dissection is continued along this border.
The aorta and the left pulmonary vein are visible.
